title: Explained: the SC's draft AI regulations, piece by piece
author: Harsh Gour
html:
What it says

  • A comprehensive explainer of the draft's structure and foundational principles
  • Notes the draft builds a careful definitional vocabulary — hallucination, black box, human-in-the-loop, explainability — before regulating any of it
  • Highlights that the 'presumption in favour of responsible adoption' frames AI as something to be embraced with care, not refused

Open questions it raises

  • How do these regulations interface with national AI governance?
  • What happens when a litigant in a district court, with no legal representation, encounters a grievance?

title: Are India's courts ready to keep the promises this draft makes?
author: Harsh Gour
html:
Key critiques

  • Institutional capacity gap: the draft assumes AI secretariats can run in-house audits, but many district courts are still building basic case-management software
  • Who governs the governors: there is no external audit of the Apex Body; the self-review provision (Reg. 57) makes the SC AI Committee judge of its own decisions
  • Training ≠ literacy: a curriculum is prescribed, but with no duration, no competency assessment, and no consequence for failure
  • Procedural compliance ≠ safety: a court can satisfy every rule and still deploy harmful AI
  • No pattern detection: incident logging is case-by-case; nothing mandates spotting systemic failures across multiple courts
  • Grievance gaps: unrepresented district-court litigants have no clear notification path or investigation mechanism
  • Comparative note: India's draft is more ambitious on paper than the UK, US, or Singapore — but weaker on the operational ecosystem underneath

title: The DPDP Act problem: told to comply with a law that doesn't cover the risk
author: M.G. Kodandaram
html:
Key critiques

  • The draft mandates compliance with the DPDP Act 2023 — but the DPDP Act is itself silent on automated decision-making and profiling
  • Unlike GDPR Article 22, Indian law gives no right not to be subjected to decisions based solely on automated processing
  • Risks of algorithmic bias, transparency failures, and errors remain legally unaddressed under current data-protection law
  • This creates a circularity problem: the regulations instruct courts to follow a data-protection law that doesn't cover the very AI risks the regulations are trying to manage

title: Human-in-the-loop must be an active design requirement
author: Vikas Mahendra · Co-founder, TERES
html:
The argument

  • The draft's human-in-the-loop principle is passive — it says final authority rests with the judge, but not how a tool must be designed to make that authority real
  • Every design choice is a HITL decision: show the judge a summary or the source? Flag uncertainty or present clean output? Require confirmation or make it easy to proceed?
  • "A passive HITL norm doesn't protect judicial independence — it creates the appearance of it. The tool must do the work."
  • TERES submitted formal comments raising 8 issues, authored by Deepika Kinhal and Subhashini Damodharan

title: The concerns and the risks: what AI displaces in complex disputes
author: Vikas Mahendra · Co-founder, TERES
html:
The concerns

  • Clients pay for judgment continuously exercised — not a polished output
  • 'Human-in-the-loop' has become near-meaningless: a human can be in the loop without making any real calls
  • The key test: "Is the human doing the load-bearing work? If not, the tool has not preserved the attorney's judgment. It has displaced it."

The risks

  • The polish problem: formatted output suppresses critical review — it's harder to find a flaw in something that looks finished
  • The confidence problem: AI strips out the uncertainty signals humans naturally convey through hedging and flagging
  • The cross-examination test: deep familiarity from working through a case cannot be replicated by retrieval — "a trial is a conversation, not a document; it rewards understanding, not retrieval"

title: Eight formal critiques submitted to the SC's AI Committee
author: Deepika Kinhal & Subhashini Damodharan · TERES
html:
The eight critiques

  1. HITL must be an active design requirement — passive framing risks rubber-stamp review under heavy caseloads
  2. AI must be confined to material on record — surfacing arguments not raised by parties introduces an independent actor into the adversarial process
  3. Explainability must include source attribution and confidence flags — without these, hallucination risk cannot be managed
  4. A common commencement deadline is needed — independent High Court dates risk regulatory arbitrage and unequal access
  5. The institutional framework needs sharper role delineation — overlapping Apex Body, five committees, and CoRE-AI risks unclear accountability
  6. Risk categorisation must be formally defined — 'High-Risk AI' is used without definition; a tiered hierarchy is needed
  7. AI-generated content verification needs independent court-side capacity — resource-poor parties cannot contest AI filings from large institutional litigants
  8. Selective deployment risks entrenching inequality — AI in some courts but not others creates a two-tier justice system

title: Four gaps to close before the framework is finalised
author: Varun Mehta & Ashirbad Nayak · Cyril Amarchand Mangaldas
html:
The four gaps

  1. In-house audit restriction: prohibiting source-code sharing with third parties contradicts the global consensus that external independent review is what builds public trust
  2. Grievance mechanism underdeveloped: no timelines, evidence standards, or compensation for harm; UNESCO's contestability safeguards — the right to challenge, the right to human review — should be incorporated
  3. Explainability unstated as a technical standard: minimum requirements per the NIST framework should be prescribed (source evidence, user-comprehensible explanations, explanations reflecting the actual process)
  4. Training obligations lack binding standards: no minimum content, frequency, or compliance accountability — they risk remaining aspirational

title: How India's draft stacks up against the EU, US, and China
author: Sahaj Sankaran
html:
Three global models

  • EU (governance model): AI in courts is classified 'high-risk' — mandatory risk management, human oversight, auditability, GDPR overlap
  • US (guidance model): no nationwide framework; a patchwork of bar-association and local-court guidance, still fragmented
  • China (smart-courts model): the most ambitious — AI used for similar-case prediction and sentencing recommendations, driven by consistency and backlog reduction

Where India lands

  • Emphasises oversight like the EU; places responsibility on individuals like the US — but unlike China, does not envision AI playing any substantive adjudicatory role
  • Key observation: no jurisdiction seeks to completely exclude AI — the debate everywhere is how to ensure human judgment stays in control

title: Community comments: transparent, technology-neutral, future-ready
author: Harshith Viswanath, Yavisth Makkar, Uddhav Gupta & Mohit Mokal
html:
The process and the asks

  • Hosted an AMA session with Mohit Mokal (Founder, Mohit Mokal Mediation), bringing the legal and legaltech community together before submitting formal comments
  • The submission draws on community discussions and independent research
  • Recommendations focus on making the framework transparent, technology-neutral, and future-ready, centred on judicial independence, accountability, and public trust

title: Five structural fixes: audits, scope, risk tiers, expertise, and objective boundaries
author: TrustBridge
html:
The five recommendations

  1. Strengthen audits, enforcement, and redressal through mandatory legal impact assessments and clearer disciplinary liabilities
  2. Clarify the scope of application by appending a definitive list of covered judicial and statutory bodies
  3. Create a distinct regulatory tier for high-risk applications that fall between permissible and prohibited uses
  4. Ensure the AI Committee and AI Secretariat include full-time technical and data-science expertise
  5. Introduce objective risk boundaries to limit subjective waivers of human-verification duties
